title: The Income Mix Most Indigenous-Owned Enterprises Have Never Mapped
linkedin:
The most common financial stress in Indigenous-owned enterprises is not a revenue problem.

It is a planning problem. Specifically, the challenge of running two income streams that behave completely differently, in the same bank account, with the same team.

NAIDOC Week this week recognises everything that has been built. Across Queensland, NSW, and Victoria, Indigenous-owned enterprises are delivering real commercial activity alongside funded programs and community services — professional services firms, construction businesses, healthcare providers, social enterprises with commercial arms, primary producers.

The financial challenge these organisations share is this: government grant income and commercial revenue do not behave the same way. Grants arrive on a funder's schedule, cover specific activities, and require acquittal reporting. Commercial revenue moves at market pace and carries no conditions. When both streams flow through the same accounts without being separated, the organisation can look financially comfortable while the commercial side is quietly underperforming.

At the start of FY27, the practical discipline is to separate the two pictures. What does commercial revenue generate on its own? What does it cost to earn? What margin remains? That picture, taken independently of grant income, reveals whether the business is genuinely building commercial sustainability or relying on funded programs to carry core costs.

The organisations in the strongest position are the ones where commercial revenue covers the operating base and grants extend mission capacity. Getting there takes deliberate financial planning, and July is exactly when that planning is most worth doing.

Full piece on the ProfitPulse blog.

#AustralianBusiness #SMEFinance #FractionalCFO
facebook:
Running a business with a mix of grant income and commercial revenue is genuinely complex. Most owners have a clear sense of what is funded, but a less clear picture of how the commercial side performs on its own.

The start of a new financial year is a natural moment to look at that separately. Which activities generate real margin? Which are quietly sustained by grant income rather than commercial performance?

For NAIDOC Week, we've published a piece on exactly this question for Indigenous-owned enterprises and social enterprises. If that is your world, or someone you know, it might be worth a read.

#AustralianBusiness #IndigenousBusiness
google:
New on the ProfitPulse Blog: Financial Planning for Indigenous-Owned Enterprises

With the start of the new financial year and NAIDOC Week this week, we have published a piece specifically for Indigenous-owned enterprises and social enterprises across Queensland, NSW, and Victoria.

The focus is a practical financial challenge that comes with running a business that has both grant-funded programs and commercial activities. These two income streams behave very differently, and managing them as a single pool can make it hard to understand how the commercial side of the business is actually performing.

In the piece, we cover what restricted and unrestricted income mean in practice and why separating them changes the financial picture, how the start of FY27 is the natural moment to build a commercial revenue plan that stands on its own, and what financial independence looks like for a purpose-led enterprise.

If you run or advise an Indigenous-owned enterprise, a social enterprise, or any purpose-led business with a mix of funded programs and commercial revenue, this piece was written for you.

Read the full piece on the ProfitPulse blog.
